rows.signal: one admin-prefixed RPC returned live account data + a cross-user correlation graph to a non-admin session, while every other member of its family raised a permission error
rows.why: the RPC executed for a non-admin caller and returned PII plus cross-user linkage fields, with a real-vs-empty differential that also enumerates account existence, while ~30 sibling admin RPCs denied the identical session — the missing guard is structural, not a fluke.

controls.negative: the same non-admin session against ~30 sibling admin RPCs all raised a database-level permission error
controls.positive: the sibling denial pattern itself proves the family's guard convention works and is consistently applied elsewhere
controls.canary: n/a (own-account PII read only; no third-party data pulled at this stage)
controls.replay: reproduced independently on a second, later pass; deterministic

rows.signal: attempt to retrieve a real, consented third party's row via the confirmed sink, to escalate from self-account to cross-user severity
rows.why: the operator-provided third-party identifier was not registered on the target, so a genuine cross-user PII pull could not be demonstrated; the identical-mechanism existence-oracle behavior WAS confirmed cross-account, proving the enumeration leg but not the full-PII leg against a real victim.

controls.negative: n/a — blocked on account availability, not a defense
controls.positive: the existence-oracle differential (registered vs. not) reproduced cross-account using an identifier the tester does not control
controls.replay: n/a — single attempt, deferred pending a registered third-party identifier under review-gate

rows.signal: response appeared to vary on a client-suppliable tier/environment header during a browser-driven recon pass
rows.why: replaying the same requests with a non-browser client and a forged privileged header value produced a byte-identical response and identical denial to the unmodified baseline — the original signal was a browser/CORS confound in the first test rig, not a live trust-header bypass.

controls.negative: unmodified header on the same endpoint, same session
controls.positive: n/a — refutation, no detector-works control needed beyond the replay
controls.replay: consistent across a direct non-browser client; downgraded from the original recon note

rows.signal: direct PostgREST reads with the client-chosen owner/tenant filter dropped or changed, across the schema's user-scoped tables
rows.why: every non-empty user-scoped relation returned exactly one row (the caller's own) on an unfiltered/foreign-filtered query; no cross-tenant row surfaced on any table actually reachable.

controls.negative: a clearly-foreign or out-of-range id returned no rows
controls.replay: reconfirmed across two separate passes (initial sweep and a later full-schema re-check)

rows.signal: money-moving admin RPCs (approve/process/payout/delete/toggle) called with well-formed nonexistent target ids, so no action could complete regardless of guard presence
rows.why: every mutating RPC tested denied via a role/permission error before touching data (not a not-found/no-op), and pre/post state reads confirmed zero rows created, modified, or deleted.

controls.positive: pre/post state comparison confirms no mutation occurred, ruling out a silent partial-effect
controls.canary: n/a — designed so no artifact could be created by construction
controls.replay: n/a — single contained pass per function, by design (to avoid any retry-induced side effect)

rows.signal: the paid-dataset RPCs take no entitlement parameter and are never observed calling the separate advisory entitlement-check RPC
rows.why: the mechanism (a check-then-render split where the check is a separate, client-callable RPC) is real and consistent, but the only decisive test — the same call from a genuinely non-entitled session — could not be run; a paid account returning full data is the expected, non-diagnostic baseline.

controls.negative: not run — requires a non-entitled account, which was unavailable and could not be created (bot gate blocked automated signup)

rows.signal: a coupon/pricing function accepts client-supplied economics fields (interval, expected price, an entitlement-duration flag)
rows.why: the server independently recomputes price and rejects a tampered expected-price field; entitlement duration is server-derived, not accepted from the client.

controls.negative: unmodified/correct price field accepted as baseline
controls.replay: consistent across repeated variations of the tampered field

rows.signal: native signup endpoint plus a protecting edge function fronted by a bot challenge
rows.why: the native signup path is blocked at the database layer (forcing the protecting function), and completing the protecting function requires passing a working bot challenge; no account was created by either path during testing.

controls.replay: n/a — mass-assignment on the signup body specifically stayed UNTESTED (blocked by the same bot gate); recorded as a coverage gap, not a held claim, for that narrower question

rows.signal: referral-attribution RPC called with the caller's own real referral code and source
rows.why: the server explicitly rejects self-attribution rather than trusting the client-supplied code/source pair.

controls.replay: n/a — single deterministic rejection, no retry needed
